commits.message:
mit-styling-aj1.7.3: the turntable — a specimen glyph scrubbed frame by frame (#26)

Rethink the frame-sequence node from scratch (the old painter's plain
colored rectangles were rejected by bmf). The canvas now turns the printer's
showpiece glyph — the ampersand — on a Y-axis spindle, QUANTIZED into 48
pre-rendered frames: the integer frame, not raw scroll, drives the picture,
so the form's defining fact (scroll is an index into a finite sequence) is
real and visible — a flip-book, not a tween.

A film ribbon with sprocket perforations lights up frame-by-frame to the
playhead, and a tracked-mono catalogue label reads the real frame index
(frame 24/48 - turntable) — an honest readout, nothing fabricated. Past 90
the negative cos scale mirrors the glyph automatically (a pale jade-lit
turned page); a confident jade edge sliver carries the thin edge-on moment.

Wears the gallery's shared specimen frame (matte jade crop marks + mono
catalogue label, no bloom) for the cohesive through-line; its mechanic (the
only canvas turntable + film strip) duplicates no sibling. paint(p,n) stays
pure scroll-driven — no timers/WAAPI — so it scrubs cleanly and needs no
extra reduced-motion gating. Font set via the canvas font string (Georgia),
sidestepping the serif-quote silent-fallback trap.

Verified by eye in Chrome across the full scroll range (frames 01..48):
front face, edge-on, back face, terminal; zero console errors (only the
environmental favicon 404).
